sections.title: Startup Benchmark
sections.description: Measures cold vs warm AgentOrc initialization time (database open, WAL, vector schema, embedding/LLM health probes).

details.definition: New AgentOrc instance + init() against a freshly created empty SQLite file each iteration
New AgentOrc instance + init() reopening an existing populated database

sections.extraMarkdown:
#### Startup methodology

- **Cold**: empty DB created per iteration — migrations + vector schema + provider probes.
- **Warm**: reopen existing DB with data already present.
- Both paths include embedding and LLM `validate()` probes via the configured provider.

sections.title: Compression Benchmark
sections.description: Measures AgentOrc.compress() duration, compression ratio, memory counts before/after, and on-disk storage saved.

rows.name: Active Memory Reduction

details.summaryPreview:
Compressed agent memory summary covering related operational notes. Key themes extracted from 2 messages: billing, meetings, deployments, and follow-ups. Source snippet: You are a memory compression engine for multi-agent systems.
Given rel
details.note: Archived source memories remain on disk with lineage; total row count typically increases by 1 (summary).

rows.notes: Archived memories remain on disk. Storage size does not shrink. Only the active working memory is reduced.

rows.name: Active Memory Reduction

details.summaryPreview:
Compressed agent memory summary covering related operational notes. Key themes extracted from 2 messages: billing, meetings, deployments, and follow-ups. Source snippet: You are a memory compression engine for multi-agent systems.
Given rel
details.note: Archived source memories remain on disk with lineage; total row count typically increases by 1 (summary).

rows.notes: Archived memories remain on disk. Storage size does not shrink. Only the active working memory is reduced.

sections.extraMarkdown:
#### Compression methodology

- Populates one agent with N memories, then calls `compress({ agent, limit })`.
- **Active-set reduction** = archived / (archived + 1 summary).
- Source memories are archived (not deleted), so total DB rows usually grow by one summary row.
- Storage saved may be 0 or negative because archival retains rows and adds a summary.

sections.title: Concurrency Benchmark
sections.description: Runs N concurrent AgentOrc.remember() writers against one client and measures throughput, failures, and average latency.

details.note: AgentOrc serializes writes via an internal AsyncMutex; concurrent callers measure queueing + work.
AgentOrc serializes writes via an internal AsyncMutex; concurrent callers measure queueing + work.
AgentOrc serializes writes via an internal AsyncMutex; concurrent callers measure queueing + work.

sections.extraMarkdown:
#### Concurrency methodology

- Levels: 10, 50, 100 concurrent writers × 10 writes each.
- All writers share one initialized `AgentOrc` instance.
- Throughput counts successful `remember()` calls only.

sections.title: Memory Usage Benchmark
sections.description: Reports Node.js process.memoryUsage() (heapUsed, rss, heapTotal) before/after init and after progressive inserts.
sections.extraMarkdown:
#### Memory methodology

- Uses `process.memoryUsage()` for heapUsed, rss, heapTotal, external, and arrayBuffers.
- Optional `node --expose-gc` enables forced GC between stages for cleaner deltas.

sections.title: Database Size Benchmark
sections.description: Measures SQLite file size (including WAL/SHM when present) after inserting 100 / 1k / 10k / 100k memories via the SDK.
sections.extraMarkdown:
#### Database size methodology

- Uses the shared SDK-populated corpus for each dataset size.
- Size includes main `.db` plus `-wal` / `-shm` if present.
- Also records `stats().databaseSizeBytes` from the SDK.

sections.title: Search Benchmark
sections.description: Populates the database first (shared corpus), then benchmarks AgentOrc.recall() with realistic natural-language queries. Reports average, p95, and p99 latency.

rows.name: Search

queries: Follow ups with partners this week

rows.notes: Semantic search over 100 memories with realistic queries

rows.name: Search

queries: Follow ups with partners this week

rows.notes: Semantic search over 1000 memories with realistic queries

rows.name: Search

queries: Follow ups with partners this week

rows.notes: Semantic search over 10000 memories with realistic queries

rows.name: Search

queries: Follow ups with partners this week

rows.notes: Semantic search over 100000 memories with realistic queries

sections.extraMarkdown:
#### Search methodology

- Shared on-disk corpus is built once per dataset size and reused across search / retrieval / filesize.
- Each sample embeds the query then runs KNN via sqlite-vec through `recall()`.
- Queries used: `Which invoices were paid recently?`, `Meetings scheduled with OpenAI`, `PostgreSQL adapter fixes`, `LangChain integration research`, `Customer refund requests`, `Production deployment plans`, `Project roadmap updates`, `Billing issues and invoice disputes`, `Recall latency incidents`, `Follow ups with partners this week`, `Who requested a status update?`, `Security or compliance meetings`

sections.title: Retrieval Benchmark
sections.description: Benchmarks top-5 / top-10 / top-20 recall against 1k, 10k, and 100k memory corpora.
sections.extraMarkdown:
#### Retrieval methodology

- Reuses the shared corpus per dataset size; topK ∈ {5, 10, 20}.
- Latency includes query embedding + vector KNN + row hydration.

sections.title: Insert Benchmark
sections.description: Measures AgentOrc.remember() total time, throughput, and average latency while inserting N realistic memories through the full SDK path (embed → persist → vector index).

rows.name: Insert
rows.notes: Batch insert of 100 memories via AgentOrc.remember()

rows.name: Insert
rows.notes: Batch insert of 1000 memories via AgentOrc.remember()

rows.name: Insert
rows.notes: Batch insert of 10000 memories via AgentOrc.remember()

rows.name: Insert
rows.notes: Batch insert of 100000 memories via AgentOrc.remember()

sections.extraMarkdown:
#### Insert methodology

- Each dataset size uses a fresh SQLite file.
- Total time measures a sequential batch of `remember()` calls (full SDK: embedding + ACID write + vector index).
- Tinybench additionally samples single-insert latency on the warm database after the batch.
